papers.title: Velocity limits in the thermonuclear supernova ejection scenario for hypervelocity stars and the origin of US 708

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVS) are a class of stars moving at high enough velocities to be gravitationally unbound from the Galaxy. Ejection from a close binary system in which one of the components undergoes a thermonuclear supernova (SN) has emerged as a promising candidate production mechanism for the least massive specimens of this class. This study presents a thorough theoretical analysis of candidate progenitor systems of thermonuclear SNe in the single degenerate helium donor scenario in the relevant parameter space leading to the ejection of HVS. The primary goal is investigation of the, previously unclear, characteristics of the velocity spectra of the ejected component. Presented are the results of 390 binary model sequences computed with the MESA framework, investigating the evolution of supernova progenitors composed of a helium-rich hot subdwarf and a accreting white dwarf. Results are then correlated with an idealized kinematic analysis of the observed object US 708. It is seen that the ejection velocity spectra reach a maximum in the range $0.19~M_\\\\odot < M_{HVS} < 0.25~M_\\\\odot$. Depending on the local Galactic potential, all donors below $0.4~\\\\text{M}_\\\\odot$ are expected to become HVS. This channel is able to account for runaway velocities up to $\\\\sim1150~\\\\text{km s}^{-1}$ with a Chandrasekhar mass accretor, exceeding $1200~\\\\text{km s}^{-1}$ if super-Chandrasekhar mass detonations are taken into account. It is found that the previously assumed mass of $0.3~M_\\\\odot$ for US 708, combined with more recently obtained proper motions, favor a sub-Chandrasekhar mass explosion with a terminal WD mass between $1.1~M_\\\\odot$ and $1.2~M_\\\\odot$. The presence of clear ejection velocity maxima provides constraints on the terminal state of a supernova progenitor. It is possible to discern certain types of explosion mechanisms from the inferred ejection velocities alone.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A comprehensive MESA-based theoretical study of 390 binary models in the single degenerate helium donor scenario reveals a peak in ejection velocity spectra between 0.19–0.25 M⊙, with maximum velocities up to ∼1150 km s⁻¹ (exceeding 1200 km s⁻¹ for super-Chandrasekhar detonations). The velocity distribution shows a clear, accretor-mass-dependent maximum independent of initial conditions, enabling inference of explosion type: for US 708, current proper motions favor a sub-Chandrasekhar explosion with a terminal white dwarf mass of 1.1–1.2 M⊙, ruling out a Chandrasekhar-mass explosion unless the star exceeds 0.34 M⊙. This establishes a direct link between ejection velocity spectra and supernova progenitor terminal states, suggesting that astrometric data alone may distinguish explosion mechanisms in future HVS studies.

source_fields.title: Velocity limits in the thermonuclear supernova ejection scenario for hypervelocity stars and the origin of US 708

source_fields.sections_text: Methods: The paper proposes using the MESA binary evolution code with the Ritter (1988) mass loss prescription to simulate the orbital evolution and Roche lobe overflow of close helium-star+white dwarf binaries, focusing on donor ejection velocities under specific initial conditions and stopping criteria to identify potential hypervelocity star production mechanisms. Appendix#2: The trajectory and time-of-flight of US 708 were computed by numerically integrating the Newtonian equations of motion in a Galactic potential model, with results agreeing well with Geier et al. (2015) for a stellar mass of 0.3 M⊙. Discussion: The ejection velocities of runaway white dwarf companions are primarily determined by terminal accretor mass and donor properties, with initial orbital separation having minimal impact, and results showing consistent ejection spectra across models—though discrepancies arise due to differences in mass transfer assumptions, metallicity, rotation, and binary evolution pathways. Conclusions: This study shows that single degenerate supernovae can produce hypervelocity runaway stars with ejection velocities exceeding 1000 km/s, particularly for terminal accretor masses above 1.1 M⊙, supporting the double detonation mechanism for objects like US 708 and providing observational constraints on progenitor properties. Introduction: This paper makes a novel contribution by proposing and analyzing a supernova-driven ejection mechanism for hypervelocity stars, particularly focusing on the post-explosion evolution of remnants from double white dwarf thermonuclear supernovae, thereby offering a viable alternative to black hole interaction scenarios for explaining the origin of high-velocity stars. The case of US 708: This section presents a kinematic analysis of the helium-rich subdwarf US 708 using two sets of proper motion data (2015 and 2020) to infer mass-dependent ejection velocities and assess the consistency of a 0.3 M☉ current mass with predicted white dwarf mass and ejection velocity spectra, while noting limitations due to uncertain distances and neglected observational errors. Physical considerations: The terminal orbital velocity of a donor star in a binary system undergoing a supernova is determined by its Roche lobe radius and Keplerian dynamics, with orbital evolution primarily governed by mass transfer and gravitational wave radiation, while magnetic braking and tides are neglected due to the system's tidal locking and helium-rich composition. Ejection velocity spectra: This section establishes that ejection velocity spectra for hypervelocity runaways in the He-star+WD channel exhibit a maximum ejection velocity dependent on the terminal white dwarf mass, with observed velocities exceeding this maximum implying either a different terminal mass or a non-binary explosion mechanism. Review of applicable explosion mechanisms: This section reviews the explosion mechanisms of thermonuclear supernovae, focusing on the single degenerate scenario where a white dwarf accretes material from a helium-rich, core-burning donor star, leading to explosion and ejection of the donor as a high-velocity star, with the explosion outcome depending on the terminal mass of the white dwarf and the specific mechanism involved.

abstract: Hypervelocity stars (HVS) are a class of stars moving at high enough velocities to be gravitationally unbound from the Galaxy.
title: Velocity limits in the thermonuclear supernova ejection scenario for hypervelocity stars and the origin

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical binary evolution study using MESA simulations; mentions US 708 only as a kinematic test case, no new observational data presented.

papers.title: Core-Collapse Supernovae in Binaries as the Origin of Galactic Hyper-Runaway Stars
papers.author_names: Fraser A. Evans Mathieu Renzo Elena Maria Rossi

abstract.text: Several stars detected moving at velocities near to or exceeding the Galactic escape speed likely originated in the Milky Way disc. We quantitatively explore the `binary supernova scenario' hypothesis, wherein these `hyper-runaway' stars are ejected at large peculiar velocities when their close, massive binary companions undergo a core-collapse supernova and the binary is disrupted. We perform an extensive suite of binary population synthesis simulations evolving massive systems to determine the assumptions and parameters which most impact the ejection rate of fast stars. In a simulation tailored to eject fast stars, we find the most likely hyper-runaway star progenitor binary is composed of a massive ($\\\\sim$$30\\\\,\\\\mathrm{M_{\\\\odot}}$) primary and a $\\\\sim$$3-4\\\\,\\\\mathrm{M_{\\\\odot}}$ companion on an orbital period that shrinks to $\\\\lesssim$1 day prior to the core collapse following a common envelope phase. The black hole remnant formed from the primary must receive a natal kick $\\\\gtrsim$1000 $\\\\mathrm{km\\\\ s^{-1}}$ to disrupt the binary and eject the companion at a large velocity. We compare the fast stars produced in these simulations to a contemporary census of early-type Milky Way hyper-runaway star candidates. We find that these rare objects may be produced in sufficient number only when poorly-constrained binary evolution parameters related to the strength of post-core collapse remnant natal kicks and common envelope efficiency are adjusted to values currently unsupported -- but not excluded -- by the literature. We discuss observational implications that may constrain the existence of these putative progenitor systems.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper presents a detailed population synthesis study of binary systems where a massive primary (≈30 M☉) and a companion (≈3–4 M☉) evolve through a common envelope phase, leading to orbital shrinkage to <1 day before core-collapse supernova. A natal kick >1000 km/s from the black hole remnant is required to disrupt the binary and eject the companion at hyper-runaway velocities (≥530 km/s). Simulations show such ejections are only viable under unverified, extreme values of natal kick strength and common envelope efficiency—parameters not well-constrained by current observations—suggesting that known hyper-runaway stars may require adjustments to binary evolution physics to be explained by this mechanism.

source_fields.title: Core-Collapse Supernovae in Binaries as the Origin of Galactic Hyper-Runaway Stars

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: The fiducial model predicts only a few high-velocity ejected main sequence companions (N_f,now ≈ 8), inconsistent with observations, while the optimized model significantly increases the ejection probability of fast companions (F_f ≈ 1.77×10⁻⁵) and predicts ∼12,000 HRSs in the Milky Way today, with a peak mass of ∼10 M⊙ and progenitor masses up to ∼30 M⊙. Conclusion: Core-collapse supernovae in binary systems do not produce enough hyper-runaway stars at velocities ≥400 km s⁻¹ to significantly explain the observed population, with ejection probabilities primarily governed by common-envelope efficiency and natal kick properties. Discussion: The fiducial and optimized binary evolution models are consistent with current observational constraints on mass ratio distributions and metallicity, but neither significantly enhances the predicted number of high-velocity runaway stars ($v_{\\mathrm{ej}} > 30\\, \\mathrm{km}\\, \\mathrm{s}^{-1}$) nor the population of bound post-core-collapse binaries, indicating that binary supernova ejection remains unlikely as a major contributor to observed HRS candidates. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel mechanism for the ejection of high-velocity early-type stars from the Milky Way disc via dynamical interactions in dense stellar environments, addressing the problem of explaining the observed population of fast-moving halo stars when galactic center origins are ruled out by Gaia astrometry. Binary Population Synthesis Models: The paper proposes a population synthesis approach using the binary_c code to simulate massive binary evolution, generating and systematically varying physical parameters to identify those most influential in producing high-velocity ejected companions, with an optimized simulation designed to maximize such ejections for robust parameter sensitivity analysis. Sample Of Galactic Hyper- Runaway Star Candidates: This section describes the selection, characterization, and orbital traceback of 68 A- and B-type hyper-runaway star candidates using Gaia data and the MwPotential2014 galactic potential to identify their likely origins and disc crossing histories.

papers.title: On the ring nebulae around runaway Wolf-Rayet stars
papers.author_names: D. M.-A. Meyer L. M. Oskinova M. Pohl M. Petrov

abstract.text: Wolf-Rayet stars are advanced evolutionary stages of massive stars. Despite their large mass-loss rates and high wind velocities, none of them display a bow shock, although a fraction of them are classified as runaway. Our 2.5-D numerical simulations of circumstellar matter around a 60Mo runaway star show that the fast Wolf-Rayet stellar wind is released into a wind-blown cavity filled with various shocks and discontinuities generated throughout the precedent evolutionary phases. The resulting fast-wind slow-wind interaction leads to the formation of spherical shells of swept-up dusty material similar to those observed in near infrared 24 micron with Spitzer, and which appear to be co-moving with the runaway massive stars, regardless of their proper motion and/or the properties of the local ambient medium. We interpret bright infrared rings around runaway Wolf-Rayet stars in the Galactic plane, like WR138a, as indication of their very high initial masses and a complex evolutionary history. Stellar-wind bow shocks become faint as stars run in diluted media, therefore, our results explain the absence of detected bow shocks around Galactic Wolf-Rayet stars such as the high-latitude, very fast-moving objects WR71, WR124 and WR148. Our results show that the absence of a bow shock is consistent with a runaway nature of some Wolf-Rayet stars. This questions the in-situ star formation scenario of high-latitude Wolf-Rayet stars in favor of dynamical ejection from birth sites in the Galactic plane.

triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Paper explicitly studies runaway Wolf-Rayet stars, their kinematics, and ejection mechanisms from Galactic plane

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: Numerical models show that wind-ISM interactions around runaway Wolf-Rayet stars produce a steady-state MHD bow-shock system with a ring nebula and cold, dense shocked regions, where only the ring and stellar wind bow shock remain cool enough ($T \\leq 10^6\\,\\rm K$) to potentially harbor unsublimated dust, consistent with observations of WR 16. Conclusion: This section concludes that WR ring nebulae form through wind-interaction mechanisms in massive stars with initial masses exceeding 40 M☉, and calls for targeted multi-wavelength observations to better understand their circumstellar structures and evolutionary histories. Discussion: Simulations show that very massive runaway stars with initial masses >40 M⊙, undergoing multiple LBV eruptions and complex evolutionary phases, produce ring-like circumstellar nebulae through confined stellar winds and shock interactions, explaining the observed centering, density, and structural diversity of WR star shells, including arcs and clumps, with key performance metrics aligning well with high-velocity runaway WR stars like WR 124 and WR 148. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel observational and theoretical framework linking the morphological diversity of WR star nebulae to their runaway dynamics, resolving the long-standing puzzle of why fast-moving WR stars do not exhibit detectable bow shocks by demonstrating that their circumstellar shells are instead shaped by wind-ISM interactions and binary evolution, thereby providing a new solution to the missing observational signature of runaway WR stars. Numerical simulations: We conduct 2.5D MHD simulations of runaway Wolf-Rayet stars interacting with ionized interstellar medium, incorporating stellar evolution, radiative cooling, and dust radiative transfer to model ring nebula formation.
